question: There are many types of relationships that we use graphs to display. What chart type best communicates the 'Part-to-Whole' relationship?

children.text: The correct answer is: Bar charts

question: What chart type best communicates the 'Correlation' relationship?

children.text: The correct answer is: Scatter Plots

question: What chart type best communicates the 'Time Series' relationship?

children.text: The correct answer is: Line Charts

question: What chart types best communicates the 'Deviation' relationship?

children.text: The correct answers are: Line Charts, Bar Charts

question: What chart type best communicates the 'Distribution' relationship?

children.text: The correct answer is: Box Plot

question: What chart type best communicates the 'Nominal Comparison' relationship

children.text: The correct answer is: Bar Chart

question: What chart type best communicates the 'Geospatial' relationship?

children.text: The correct answer is: Map

question: When should we use Pie Charts?

children.text: The correct answer is: Never.

question: Why do we use contrast?

options.text: To make our computer monitor easier to read
Our eyes like it. It looks good

children.text: The correct answer is: Our eyes like it. It looks good

question: How important is alignment?

options.text: Critically important (make sure you have learned how to use PowerPoint's alignment tools)

children.text: The correct answer is: Critically important (make sure you have learned how to use PowerPoint's alignment tools)

question: How is the principle of proximity helpful?

options.text: Helps us understand how groups are created (intentionally or unintentionally)
Allows us to squeeze more onto the page
Helps us get to know people

children.text: The correct answer is: Helps us understand how groups are created (intentionally or unintentionally)

question: How many different font types should you use in a single presentation?

children.text: The correct answer is: 2

question: How many colors should we use in a presentation?

options.text: 2 more than the number of fonts

children.text: The correct answer is: 2 to 3

question: What should you do with whitespace?

options.text: Ensure you are using enough of it
Color it blue, it is prettier

children.text: The correct answer is: Ensure you are using enough of it

question: What is the bare assertion fallacy?

options.text: A premise in an argument that is assumed to be true merely because it says that it is true
Claiming to be right because you say you are right
A dream where you are presenting naked

children.text: The correct answers are: A premise in an argument that is assumed to be true merely because it says that it is true, Claiming to be right because you say you are right

question: What is graphical excellence?

children.text: The correct answers are: Honest, Multivariate, Efficient

question: Why have we repeated the principle of proximity in this lesson and the previous lesson?

options.text: Didn't notice. The course is brilliant. Carry on!
Because repetition ad nauseum helps me learn?...

children.text: The correct answer is: Didn't notice. The course is brilliant. Carry on!

question: The principle of similarity states that we tend to group things which share visual characteristics such as:

children.text: The correct answers are: Size, Shape, Color, Orientation

question: What is symmetry associated with?

children.text: The correct answers are: Stability, Consistency, Structure

question: What does the principle of connection state?

options.text: Elements that are visually connected are perceived as more related than elements with no connection
We need to connect our most important ideas
I just can't make no connection

children.text: The correct answer is: Elements that are visually connected are perceived as more related than elements with no connection

question: What is the key to making brainstorming as effective as possible?

options.text: Conduct brainstorming sessions early in the day
Don't use brainstorming, it doesn't work

children.text: The correct answer is: Engage in debate and dissent

question: What are our Cardinal Rules of brainstorming?

children.text: The correct answers are: Plan your participants, Focus on ideas, Structure the session, Establish rules in advance

question: What was the golden rule talked about in this lesson?

options.text: Facts known by the audience go in the Introduction
We are creating our presentation to answer a question in the mind of our audience
Our objective is to compel our audience to do something

children.text: The correct answer is: Create ideas first, slides second

question: Hypothetical example: We are in the finance department and are giving an update. What is the best way for us to frame our presentation?

options.text: How did we perform last quarter, and what do we need to do differently?

children.text: The correct answer is: How did we perform last quarter, and what do we need to do differently?

question: Why are we creating our presentation?

options.text: To sell our product to a customer
To answer a question in the mind of our audience
To raise money for our start-up
Becuase we have been asked to by our boss

children.text: The correct answer is: To answer a question in the mind of our audience

question: What are they four parts to our introduction?

children.text: The correct answers are: Context, Catalyst, Answer, Question

question: What is the Context part of the Introduction?

options.text: The background to the presentation that includes facts already known to your audience

children.text: The correct answer is: The background to the presentation that includes facts already known to your audience

question: What is the Catalyst portion of the Introduction?

options.text: An event or trigger, sometimes referred to as the complication
The techniques that speed-up the development of a presentation
What happened or changed that created the need for you to write this presentation

children.text: The correct answers are: An event or trigger, sometimes referred to as the complication, What happened or changed that created the need for you to write this presentation

question: What is the Question portion of the Introduction?

options.text: What we are trying to plant in the mind of the audience with the context and catalyst portions of the Introduction
The natural question that arises in the mind of the audience

children.text: The correct answers are: What we are trying to plant in the mind of the audience with the context and catalyst portions of the Introduction, The natural question that arises in the mind of the audience, The 'topic' of the presentation

question: Why is it important to follow a process to develop a presentation?

options.text: If you are really good, you don't need to follow a process!
Because creating presentations is all about left brain thinking
Because there is not such thing as creativity
Because it is very easy to focus on the wrong thing, and be led astray

children.text: The correct answer is: Because it is very easy to focus on the wrong thing, and be led astray

question: What is the 1st step of our process?

options.text: Why are we speaking to our audience (identify their question)?
How will we deliver this presentation?

children.text: The correct answer is: Who is our audience?

question: What is the 2nd step of our process?

options.text: Why are we speaking to our audience (identify their question)?
How will we deliver this presentation?

children.text: The correct answer is: Why are we speaking to our audience (identify their question)?

question: What is the 3rd step of our process?

options.text: Why are we speaking to our audience (identify their question)?
How will we deliver this presentation?

children.text: The correct answer is: What is our answer?

question: What is the 4th step of our process?

options.text: Why are we speaking to our audience (identify their question)?
How will we deliver this presentation?

children.text: The correct answer is: How will we deliver this presentation?

question: Our first step is 'The Who'. What do we mean by this?

options.text: Determine who your audience truly is. Who are you speaking to?
The Who is a famous English rock band
Determining our answer to a key question

children.text: The correct answer is: Determine who your audience truly is. Who are you speaking to?

question: The second step in our process is 'The Why'. What do we mean by this?

options.text: Determine the question inside the mind of our audience and what we want the audience to do a the end
Determine our personal objective from creating the presentation
A process of deep existential soul searching to ensure you are a confident speaker

children.text: The correct answer is: Determine the question inside the mind of our audience and what we want the audience to do a the end

question: The third step in our process is 'The What'. What does 'The What' focus on?

options.text: Themes from Biggie Smalls' debut album 'Ready to Die' which featured 'The What' on track 9
Determining what types of slides we need to create
Determining what it is we want our Audience to do as a result of the presentation
Determining the answer to the question that has been planted in the mind of the audience

children.text: The correct answer is: Determining the answer to the question that has been planted in the mind of the audience

question: The final step in our process is 'The How'. What is the focus of 'The How'?

options.text: How to answer our audience's question
This is how we do it!
How we will deliver the presentation

children.text: The correct answer is: How we will deliver the presentation

question: What are some of the fundamental elements and principles of design?

children.text: The correct answers are: Shape & Form, Color, Composition, Contrast, Line, Point, Negative Space

question: What can we do to try and set the right tone?

options.text: Send a well prepared agenda in advance
Adopt the appropriate disposition for the meeting

children.text: The correct answers are: Send a well prepared agenda in advance, Dress appropriately, Adopt the appropriate disposition for the meeting

question: What are some things you can do to manage stress?

options.text: Don't worry about the presentation until the last minute
Talk to yourself like a crazy person

children.text: The correct answers are: Quite your mind, Laugh, Prepare, Breathe

question: What body language and delivery mistakes should you be on the lookout for?

children.text: The correct answers are: Verbal ticks, Talking to the screen, Closed posture, Not displaying any emotion

question: When preparing and practicing the delivery of your presentation, what four factors should you focus on?

children.text: The correct answers are: Clarity, Pace, Engaging with the audience, Smiling and making eye contact

question: What is the first step of the recommended preparation process?

options.text: Get a good night sleep and review the script once, maybe twice before the presentation
Present to someone else. Get feedback
Run through the presentation two or three time working on length, simplifying language, and identifying likely questions
Speak the presentation out loud and improvise. Test turns of phrases, identify key points that you want to make for each phrase
Write down the verbal voice over and create a formal script
Test the length of the presentation. Revise the deck, eliminating or combining slide ideas
Run through the script a few more times and the put it aside

children.text: The correct answer is: Speak the presentation out loud and improvise. Test turns of phrases, identify key points that you want to make for each phrase

question: What is the second step of the recommended preparation process?

options.text: Get a good night sleep and review the script once, maybe twice before the presentation
Present to someone else. Get feedback
Run through the presentation two or three time working on length, simplifying language, and identifying likely questions
Speak the presentation out loud and improvise. Test turns of phrases, identify key points that you want to make for each phrase
Write down the verbal voice over and create a formal script
Test the length of the presentation. Revise the deck, eliminating or combining slide ideas
Run through the script a few more times and the put it aside

children.text: The correct answer is: Write down the verbal voice over and create a formal script

options.text: Get a good night sleep and review the script once, maybe twice before the presentation
Present to someone else. Get feedback
Run through the presentation two or three time working on length, simplifying language, and identifying likely questions
Speak the presentation out loud and improvise. Test turns of phrases, identify key points that you want to make for each phrase
Write down the verbal voice over and create a formal script
Test the length of the presentation. Revise the deck, eliminating or combining slide ideas
Run through the script a few more times and the put it aside

children.text: The correct answer is: Run through the presentation two or three time working on length, simplifying language, and identifying likely questions

options.text: Get a good night sleep and review the script once, maybe twice before the presentation
Present to someone else. Get feedback
Run through the presentation two or three time working on length, simplifying language, and identifying likely questions
Speak the presentation out loud and improvise. Test turns of phrases, identify key points that you want to make for each phrase
Write down the verbal voice over and create a formal script
Test the length of the presentation. Revise the deck, eliminating or combining slide ideas
Run through the script a few more times and the put it aside

children.text: The correct answer is: Test the length of the presentation. Revise the deck, eliminating or combining slide ideas

options.text: Get a good night sleep and review the script once, maybe twice before the presentation
Present to someone else. Get feedback
Run through the presentation two or three time working on length, simplifying language, and identifying likely questions
Speak the presentation out loud and improvise. Test turns of phrases, identify key points that you want to make for each phrase
Write down the verbal voice over and create a formal script
Test the length of the presentation. Revise the deck, eliminating or combining slide ideas
Run through the script a few more times and the put it aside

Present to someone else. Get feedback
Run through the presentation two or three time working on length, simplifying language, and identifying likely questions
Speak the presentation out loud and improvise. Test turns of phrases, identify key points that you want to make for each phrase
Write down the verbal voice over and create a formal script
Test the length of the presentation. Revise the deck, eliminating or combining slide ideas
Run through the script a few more times and the put it aside

question: What is the seventh step of the recommended preparation process?

options.text: Get a good night sleep and review the script once, maybe twice before the presentation
Present to someone else. Get feedback
Run through the presentation two or three time working on length, simplifying language, and identifying likely questions
Speak the presentation out loud and improvise. Test turns of phrases, identify key points that you want to make for each phrase
Write down the verbal voice over and create a formal script
Test the length of the presentation. Revise the deck, eliminating or combining slide ideas
Run through the script a few more times and the put it aside

children.text: The correct answer is: Get a good night sleep and review the script once, maybe twice before the presentation

question: What goes in the headline?

options.text: The main message of the slide

children.text: The correct answer is: The main message of the slide

question: What goes in the body of the slide?

options.text: The supporting evidence that supports the main message

children.text: The correct answer is: The supporting evidence that supports the main message

question: What is a swipe file?

options.text: Collection of useful slide designs and frameworks that you can utilize for inspiration
Hacker code to get free templates
Another name for a garbage can

children.text: The correct answer is: Collection of useful slide designs and frameworks that you can utilize for inspiration

question: When is the best time to use clip art?

options.text: In marketing and sales presentations, but not in finance presentations
When the clip art is of a cute cat

children.text: The correct answer is: Never

question: What elements can be repeated on all slides?

children.text: The correct answers are: Location for a headline, Location for footnotes, Location  for page numbers

question: What do we use Tornado diagrams for?

options.text: To display how several variables change over time

children.text: The correct answer is: Sensitivity analysis

question: When do we use a Bubble Chart?

options.text: When your scatter plot is ugly
When you want to show three variables

children.text: The correct answer is: When you want to show three variables

question: What chart types should we try and avoid using?

children.text: The correct answers are: Donut Chart, Pie Chart, Circle chart, Anything 3-D

question: What is the best use of a Waterfall Chart?

options.text: To show how increases and decreases in a balance affect that balance over time
As a fancy nominal comparison bar chart

children.text: The correct answer is: To show how increases and decreases in a balance affect that balance over time

question: What is one of the more common uses of a Marimekko Chart?

options.text: To show data on the Finnish textile industry
To display the composition of markets

children.text: The correct answer is: To display the composition of markets

question: What are Motion Charts used for?

options.text: To explore how several variables change over time

children.text: The correct answer is: To explore how several variables change over time

question: What is a storyboard?

options.text: A cardboard board to pin up cartoon drawings
What happens when you are subject to a boring story

children.text: The correct answer is: A blueprint of the movie

question: Who invented storyboards?

children.text: The correct answer is: Walt Disney

question: What was the great innovation of storyboarding?

options.text: Allowed film makers to edit the film before making it
The ability to draw your story

children.text: The correct answer is: Allowed film makers to edit the film before making it

question: What are the two approaches discussed in the lesson?

children.text: The correct answer is: Text-based outlining and storyboarding

question: What tools are recommended to use for storyboarding?

children.text: The correct answer is: Pen and paper

question: What is the principle of Abstraction?

options.text: A grouping principle, whereby a hierarchy is adhered to with higher levels of abstraction (less detail) placed near the top, with more specific concepts underneath
An approach whereby we simplify our question so profoundly that we reach a level of enlightenment

children.text: The correct answer is: A grouping principle, whereby a hierarchy is adhered to with higher levels of abstraction (less detail) placed near the top, with more specific concepts underneath

question: Which lists are MECE (pick 2)

options.text: Star Wars films: New Hope, Empire, Revenge of the Sith
The global population broken down into age groups of 0-20 year-olds, 21-40 year-olds, 41-60 year-olds, 61-80 year-olds, and 81 and over

children.text: The correct answers are: Profit=revenue minus expenses, The global population broken down into age groups of 0-20 year-olds, 21-40 year-olds, 41-60 year-olds, 61-80 year-olds, and 81 and over

question: What are the three Golden Rules to follow when applying the principle of abstraction and organizing your ideas?

options.text: Concepts should be arranged in the shape of a triangle
Concepts at any level must be presented in a strict logical order
Concepts in any group are always the same kind of idea
Concepts or ideas at any level of your argument must be more abstract summaries of the concepts that are grouped below

children.text: The correct answers are: Concepts at any level must be presented in a strict logical order, Concepts in any group are always the same kind of idea, Concepts or ideas at any level of your argument must be more abstract summaries of the concepts that are grouped below

question: Match the argument with whether it is deductive or inductive: 'Jill and Bob are friends. Jill likes to dance, cook and write. Bob likes to dance and cook. Therefore it can be assumed he also likes to write.

children.text: The correct answer is: Inductive

question: Match the argument with whether it is deductive or inductive: 'All dogs are mammals. All mammals have kidneys. Therefore all dogs have kidneys.

children.text: The correct answer is: Deductive

question: What is the rule of 7 (updated)?

options.text: There is no such thing as 7 or 9 of anything. We should seek to structure our ideas into groups of 4-5 or less
Organize your ideas into groups of 7

children.text: The correct answer is: There is no such thing as 7 or 9 of anything. We should seek to structure our ideas into groups of 4-5 or less

question: What are the two defining characteristics of Tables?

options.text: Information is encoded as text (words and numbers)
They are not as nice to look at as graphs
They are arranged in columns and rows

children.text: The correct answers are: Information is encoded as text (words and numbers), They are arranged in columns and rows

question: What re some of the primary benefits of a table?

options.text: Tables make it easy to look up individual values
Tables make it easy to compare pairs of related values
Textual encoding provides a level of precision that you cannot get in graphs
Tables are easier to create than graphs
Tables can handle larger data sets than graphs

children.text: The correct answers are: Tables make it easy to look up individual values, Tables make it easy to compare pairs of related values, Textual encoding provides a level of precision that you cannot get in graphs

question: What are some of the characteristics that define graphs?

options.text: Information is encoded as text (words and numbers)
Axes provide scales (quantitative and categorical) that are used to label and assign value to the visual objects
They are nicer to look at than tables
Values are encoded as visual objects in relation to the axis
Values are displayed within an area delineated by one or more axis

children.text: The correct answers are: Axes provide scales (quantitative and categorical) that are used to label and assign value to the visual objects, Values are encoded as visual objects in relation to the axis, Values are displayed within an area delineated by one or more axis

question: When should you use graphs?

options.text: When the message or story is contained in the shape of the data
When the display will be used to reveal relationships among whole sets of values
When you need to 'sex-up' a slide
When you have production support and they can create the graph for you

children.text: The correct answers are: When the message or story is contained in the shape of the data, When the display will be used to reveal relationships among whole sets of values

question: Who is the hero of our presentation?

children.text: The correct answer is: The audience

question: What is the Audience Map used for?

options.text: To be used to find your presentation venue. X marks the spot.
To help identify the main decision maker
To develop a strategic approach for engaging with your 'room'

children.text: The correct answer is: To develop a strategic approach for engaging with your 'room'

question: What are the 4 quadrants of the Audience Map?

children.text: The correct answer is: Personality, Power, Access, Resistance

question: Pick the question that corresponds with the 'Personality' quadrant

options.text: What is their style, energy level, and emotional state?
How does your audience like to consume information?

children.text: The correct answer is: What is their style, energy level, and emotional state?

question: Pick the question that corresponds with the 'Access' quadrant

options.text: How does your audience like to consume information?
What is their style, energy level, and emotional state?

children.text: The correct answer is: How does your audience like to consume information?

question: Pick the question that corresponds with the 'Power' quadrant

options.text: How does your audience like to consume information?
What is their style, energy level, and emotional state?

children.text: The correct answer is: How do decisions get made?

question: Pick the question that corresponds with the 'Resistance' quadrant

options.text: How does your audience like to consume information?
What is their style, energy level, and emotional state?

children.text: The correct answer is: Who are your 'friends in court'?

question: Why are stories like a cup?

options.text: Because they are the brain's natural container for information
Because they are simple and straightforward
Because you can put in them whatever you like
Because they are delicate, and need to be handled carefully

children.text: The correct answer is: Because they are the brain's natural container for information

question: What do stories add to our presentations? Why should be use them?

options.text: Stories lull your audience to sleep
Stories make your message more memorable

children.text: The correct answers are: Stories stop disagreement, Stories make your message more memorable, Stories increase trust

question: What characteristics make stories memorable?

children.text: The correct answers are: Concreteness, Unexpectedness, Credibility, Simplicity, Emotion

question: What is visual thinking?

options.text: The phenomenon of thinking through visual processing

children.text: The correct answer is: The phenomenon of thinking through visual processing

question: Match the type of mental processing with the characteristic: 'Conscious, sequential, and slow/hard'

children.text: The correct answer is: Attentive processing

question: Match the type of mental processing with the characteristic: 'Below the level of consciousness, very rapid'

children.text: The correct answer is: Pre-attentive processing

question: What are the visual attribute triggers of pre-attentive processing?

children.text: The correct answers are: Length, Size, Hue, Shape, Width, Orientation, Enclosure, 2D position, Intensity

question: Who is Cicero?

options.text: Some Italian dude who wasn't nearly as effective as Demosthenes

children.text: The correct answer is: Some Italian dude who wasn't nearly as effective as Demosthenes

question: What is the ultimate objective of our presentation?

options.text: To get praise for our slick PowerPoint skills

children.text: The correct answer is: To prompt action!

question: Which of the following are reasonable next steps to follow your presentation?

options.text: For your to develop a full proposal for the customer
For the customer to test your software as part of a trial
For the customer to schedule a follow-up demo with field staff

children.text: The correct answers are: For the customer to test your software as part of a trial, For the customer to schedule a follow-up demo with field staff

question: Where should the next steps go in your presentation?

options.text: In your introduction, that is why you need to identify them at the beginning
At the end of the presentation, but we need to identify their nature early as it might inform the development of the rest of the presentation

children.text: The correct answer is: At the end of the presentation, but we need to identify their nature early as it might inform the development of the rest of the presentation
